result.markdown:
--- title: "Relationships I" subtitle: "POL51" author: "Haley Daarstad" institute: "University of California, Davis" date: today date-format: long format: revealjs: html-math-method: mathjax chalkboard: true slide-number: true height: 900 width: 1600 code-line-numbers: true smooth-scrawl: true theme: [simple, html/custom.scss] incremental: true title-slide-attributes: data-background-image: img/dubois-spiral-2.png data-background-size: contain data-background-position: right data-background-color: "#dc354a" --- # Plan for today {.center background-color="#dc354a"} . . . Data, objects, variables . . . Mean, median, variance . . . Summarizing data # Data, objects, variables . . . Objects are one of the most confusing parts of coding for new programmers . . . An **object** is a "thing" that lives in R that we can use; think of the pantry metaphor! . . . In this class, 99.99% of objects are just **data** . . . Data has **variables** which are represented as columns . . . Objects ➡️ Data ➡️ Variables ## Data, objects, variables . . . `trade` is a data object that contains variables like **imports** ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} trade ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 11,493 × 10 country year imports exports gdp pop land_borders sea_borders 1 United States … 1946 5000 9775 2.48e12 1.38e8 2 1 2 United States … 1947 5824 15369 2.43e12 1.40e8 2 1 3 United States … 1948 7710 12470 2.49e12 1.43e8 2 1 4 United States … 1949 7160 11920 2.47e12 1.47e8 2 1 5 United States … 1950 9140 10220 2.56e12 1.49e8 2 1 6 United States … 1951 11400 14920 2.73e12 1.51e8 2 1 7 United States … 1952 11220 14950 2.82e12 1.53e8 2 1 8 United States … 1953 11460 15330 2.99e12 1.56e8 2 1 9 United States … 1954 10670 14690 3.00e12 1.59e8 2 1 10 United States … 1955 11850 15410 3.10e12 1.62e8 2 1 # ℹ 11,483 more rows # ℹ 2 more variables: min_cap_dist , sum_igos ``` ::: ::: . . . `trade_2010` and `trade` are both objects, `year` is a variable ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} trade_2010 = trade |> filter(year == 2010) ``` ::: ## Data, objects, variables Confusing! `imports_gdp` is an object that contains a variable that's also named `imports_gdp` ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} imports_gdp = trade |> mutate(imports_gdp = imports / gdp) ``` ::: . . .
This is why we need to pick good variable names! ## A common mistake Say we wanted to multiply GDP per capita by population and plot the result: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} gap_gdp = gapminder |> mutate(gdp = gdpPercap * pop) ``` ::: . . . ❌ ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(gapminder, aes(x = year, y = gap_gdp)) + geom_point() ``` ::: Mistake: using the original gapminder data, treating `gap_gdp` as a **variable** . . . ✅ ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(gap_gdp, aes(x = year, y = gdp)) + geom_point() ``` ::: Correct: use the new data object, which contains the newly created variable `gdp` # Summarizing data {background-color="#dc354a"} ## Summarizing data . . . Often, with data, we want to **compare** things . . . Are democracies more peaceful than autocracies? . . . Does early-voting increase turnout? . . . To do this, we often rely on **statistical summaries** of our data: . . . Means, medians, minimums, maximums, variances ## Summary stats {.center} . . . The *mean*: 3, 4, 8 $\rightarrow$ $\frac{(3 + 4 + 8)}{3} = 5$ . . . The *median*: 3, **4**, 8 $\rightarrow$ 4 . . . The *minimum*: **3**, 4, 8 $\rightarrow$ 3 . . . The *maximum*: 3, 4, **8** $\rightarrow$ 8 ## Example: an experiment Some counties let people vote **early**; others don't . . . Do counties with early provisions have **higher** turnout? ## The data ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} | Vote early? | Turnout (%) | |:-----------:|:-----------:| | Yes | 54.7 | | Yes | 56.7 | | No | 37.3 | | No | 44.1 | | No | 40.6 | | No | 38.6 | | Yes | 46.7 | | No | 35.4 | | Yes | 51.8 | | Yes | 50.1 | ::: ::: ## Quantifying "higher" We can compare the *averages* of counties that do have early voting against those that don't: . . . ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} |Vote early? | Average turnout| |:-----------|---------------:| |No | 40.1| |Yes | 50.2| ::: ::: . . . Counties with early voting have, **on average**, 10 percentage points higher turnout ## Mean vs. median We almost always compare averages, but medians are helpful in some cases: . . . ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-5_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-9-1.png){fig-align='center' width=80%} ::: ::: ::: notes What's happening here? ::: ## One more stat: variation {.center} . . . Another way to compare data is by how much a variable *varies* . . . Some distributions are very *narrow*, others are very *wide* . . . Narrow = observations are similar; wide = observations are not similar . . . ## Measuring variation: the standard deviation The formula: $$ s = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^N (x_i - \overline{x})^2}$$ . . . All the movement is here, the sum of the differences between each observation $x_i$ and the average observation $\overline{x}$ $$\sum_{i=1}^N (x_i - \overline{x})^2$$ ## Variance intuition ::::::: columns :::: {.column width="50%"} ::: fragment ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Wide distribution
age \(\overline{age}\) \(\ age - \overline{age}\) \(\ (age - \overline{age})^2 \)
87 55.3 31.7 1003.6
41 55.3 -14.3 205.1
23 55.3 -32.3 1044.6
45 55.3 -10.3 106.5
58 55.3 2.7 7.2
````` ::: ::: ::: :::: :::: {.column width="50%"} ::: fragment ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Narrow distribution
age \(\overline{age}\) \(\ age - \overline{age}\) \(\ (age - \overline{age})^2 \)
48 49.9 -1.9 3.8
48 49.9 -1.9 3.8
52 49.9 2.1 4.2
48 49.9 -1.9 3.8
50 49.9 0.1 0.0
````` ::: ::: ::: :::: ::::::: $$\sum_{i=1}^N (age_i - \overline{age})^2$$ . . . The farther observations are from the mean, the **larger** the standard deviation ## Measuring variation Same average (\~\$50,000), different **standard deviations** ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-5_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-12-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: ::: notes What could we measure with sd? Can we use standard deviation to measure *equality*? ::: # Summarizing data in R {background-color="#dc354a"} ## Summary statistics with `summarize()` We can use `summarize()` to **calculate** things like the mean, median, etc. ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](img/summarize.png){fig-align='center' width=50%} ::: ::: . . . Summarizing **condenses** data into statistics ## Using `summarize()` Say I want to know the average life expectancy in the world in 2007: . . . ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} gapminder %>% filter(year == 2007) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 142 × 6 country continent year lifeExp pop gdpPercap 1 Afghanistan Asia 2007 43.8 31889923 975. 2 Albania Europe 2007 76.4 3600523 5937. 3 Algeria Africa 2007 72.3 33333216 6223. 4 Angola Africa 2007 42.7 12420476 4797. 5 Argentina Americas 2007 75.3 40301927 12779. 6 Australia Oceania 2007 81.2 20434176 34435. 7 Austria Europe 2007 79.8 8199783 36126. 8 Bahrain Asia 2007 75.6 708573 29796. 9 Bangladesh Asia 2007 64.1 150448339 1391. 10 Belgium Europe 2007 79.4 10392226 33693. # ℹ 132 more rows ``` ::: ::: ## Using `summarize()` ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} gapminder %>% filter(year == 2007) %>% summarize() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 1 × 0 ``` ::: ::: ## Using `summarize()` ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} gapminder %>% filter(year == 2007) %>% summarize(avg_life = mean(lifeExp)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 1 × 1 avg_life 1 67.0 ``` ::: ::: Notice how the data has been *summarized*: only one row . . . Notice that I've *named* the summary statistic `avg_life` ## Using `summarize()` What if I also wanted to know the **median** life expectancy? . . . ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} gapminder %>% filter(year == 2007) %>% summarize(avg_life = mean(lifeExp), med_life = median(lifeExp)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 1 × 2 avg_life med_life 1 67.0 71.9 ``` ::: ::: Notice! how similar this looks to `mutate()` ## Using `summarize()` What if I also wanted to know the **min** and **max** life expectancy? . . . ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} gapminder %>% filter(year == 2007) %>% summarize(avg_life = mean(lifeExp), med_life = median(lifeExp), min_life = min(lifeExp), max_life = max(lifeExp)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 1 × 4 avg_life med_life min_life max_life 1 67.0 71.9 39.6 82.6 ``` ::: ::: ## Using `summarize()` What if I also wanted to know the **standard deviation** of life expectancy? . . . ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} gapminder %>% filter(year == 2007) %>% summarize(avg_life = mean(lifeExp), med_life = median(lifeExp), min_life = min(lifeExp), max_life = max(lifeExp), sd_life = sd(lifeExp)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 1 × 5 avg_life med_life min_life max_life sd_life 1 67.0 71.9 39.6 82.6 12.1 ``` ::: ::: ## Using `summarize()` ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} gapminder %>% filter(year == 2007) %>% summarize(avg_life = mean(lifeExp), med_life = median(lifeExp), min_life = min(lifeExp), max_life = max(lifeExp), sd_life = sd(lifeExp)) ``` ::: . . . `summarize()` is like `mutate()` in that you are creating *new variables* . . . So note that you have to *name* the new variables! ## We can use summary functions in filter which country had the lowest life expectancy in 2007? . . . ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} gapminder %>% filter(year == 2007) %>% filter(lifeExp == min(lifeExp)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 1 × 6 country continent year lifeExp pop gdpPercap 1 Swaziland Africa 2007 39.6 1133066 4513. ``` ::: ::: ## We can use summary functions in filter which countries had above average GPD but below average health? . . . ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} gapminder %>% filter(year == 2007) %>% filter(gdpPercap > mean(gdpPercap), lifeExp < mean(lifeExp)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 3 × 6 country continent year lifeExp pop gdpPercap 1 Botswana Africa 2007 50.7 1639131 12570. 2 Equatorial Guinea Africa 2007 51.6 551201 12154. 3 Gabon Africa 2007 56.7 1454867 13206. ``` ::: ::: ## Woe unto us: missing data . . . In life, sometimes data is **missing** . . . respondent doesn't answer a question, no one knows Afghanistan's GDP in 1943, etc. . . . R represents missing data as **NA** . . . ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} |name |type1 |type2 | height_m| weight_kg| |:--------|:------|:------|--------:|---------:| |Lycanroc |rock |NA | NA| NA| |Vulpix |fire |ice | NA| NA| |Muk |poison |poison | NA| NA| |Persian |normal |dark | NA| NA| |Marowak |ground |fire | NA| NA| |Graveler |rock |ground | NA| NA| ::: ::: ## Summarizing with missing data If you try to *summarize* data that has missing values, R will return `NA`: . . . ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} pokemon %>% summarise(average_height = mean(height_m)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 1 × 1 average_height 1 NA ``` ::: ::: . . . ::: callout-note Any math operation with `NA` will return `NA` (.e.g, 3 + `NA` = `NA`) ::: ## What to do? . . . There are whole classes on what to do with missing data (impute, simulate, model, etc.) . . . In this class we will tell R to ignore missing observations . . . We can do this by adding the `na.rm = TRUE` argument to summary ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} pokemon %>% summarise(average_height = mean(height_m, na.rm = TRUE)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 1 × 1 average_height 1 1.16 ``` ::: ::: ## Remember... . . . If you're trying to calculate a summary statistic and keep getting `NA`... . . . 99.99999% of the time you need to add `na.rm = TRUE` to your function! . . . ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} pokemon %>% summarise(average_height = mean(height_m, na.rm = TRUE)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 1 × 1 average_height 1 1.16 ``` ::: ::: ## 🚨 Your turn: 🚢 trade 🚢 🚨 Dataset from `juanr` on international trade: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} Table: Trade patterns around the world |country | year| imports| exports| gdp| pop| land_borders| sea_borders| min_cap_dist| sum_igos| |:------------------------|----:|-------:|-------:|------------:|---------:|------------:|-----------:|------------:|--------:| |United States of America | 1946| 5000| 9775| 2.479307e+12| 138170640| 2| 1| 0| 44| |United States of America | 1947| 5824| 15369| 2.430213e+12| 139838676| 2| 1| 0| 47| |United States of America | 1948| 7710| 12470| 2.494227e+12| 143378709| 2| 1| 0| 52| |United States of America | 1949| 7160| 11920| 2.466941e+12| 146714635| 2| 1| 0| 57| |United States of America | 1950| 9140| 10220| 2.562489e+12| 149230105| 2| 1| 0| 59| |United States of America | 1951| 11400| 14920| 2.729119e+12| 150880698| 2| 1| 0| 60| ::: ::: ## 🚨 Your turn: 🚢 trade 🚢 🚨 Using the `trade` dataset: 1. How has Inter-Governmental Organizations (IGOs) membership expanded over time? Calculate the *median* number of IGOs that countries belonged to in 1960. Repeat for 2010. About how much has IGO membership grown in 50 years? 2. What is having a sea border worth? Look only at countries that have at least one sea border in the year 2010. What is the average value of exports among these countries? Do the same for countries with no sea borders. About how much more do countries with sea borders export than countries without? 3. What was the country with the *most* exports in 1960? What about in 2012? ::: callout-tip 1. Remember to attach your libraries using `library()` 2. Trade is in the `juanr` package and these functions are found in the `tidyverse` package, also run the `library(dyplr)` after these two (this will already be installed) 3. `?trade` to look at the variables and `distinct()` to look at a variables values ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html} ``` ::: ::: ## 🚨 🚢 trade Q1 🚢 🚨 {.smaller} ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} library(juanr) library(tidyverse) library(dplyr) trade_1960 <- trade |> filter(year == 1960) |> summarise( median_igos = median(sum_igos, na.rm = TRUE) ) trade_2010 <- trade |> filter(year == 2010) |> summarise( median_igos = median(sum_igos, na.rm = TRUE) ) trade_1960 ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 1 × 1 median_igos 1 33 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} trade_2010 ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 1 × 1 median_igos 1 66 ``` ::: ::: ## 🚨 🚢 trade Q2 🚢 🚨 ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} trade_seaborders <- trade |> filter(sea_borders >= 1 & year == 2010) |> summarise( avg_exports = mean(exports, na.rm = TRUE) ) trade_no_seaborders <- trade |> filter(sea_borders == 0 & year == 2010) |> summarise( avg_exports = mean(exports, na.rm = TRUE) ) trade_seaborders ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 1 × 1 avg_exports 1 105389. ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} trade_no_seaborders ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 1 × 1 avg_exports 1 31408. ``` ::: ::: ## 🚨 🚢 trade Q3 🚢 🚨 {.smaller} ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} ## Option 1 trade_exp1960 <- trade |> filter(year == 1960) |> filter(exports == max(exports, na.rm=TRUE)) trade_exp2012 <- trade |> filter(year == 2012) |> filter(exports == max(exports, na.rm=TRUE)) trade_exp1960 ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 1 × 10 country year imports exports gdp pop land_borders sea_borders 1 United States o… 1960 16171. 20536 3.60e12 1.76e8 2 2 # ℹ 2 more variables: min_cap_dist , sum_igos ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} trade_exp2012 ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 1 × 10 country year imports exports gdp pop land_borders sea_borders 1 China 2012 2331123. 2494240. 1.79e13 1310926531. 14 4 # ℹ 2 more variables: min_cap_dist , sum_igos ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} ## Option 2 trade_exp1960 <- trade |> filter(year == 1960) |> arrange(desc(exports)) trade_exp2012 <- trade |> filter(year == 2012) |> arrange(desc(exports)) trade_exp1960[1,] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 1 × 10 country year imports exports gdp pop land_borders sea_borders 1 United States o… 1960 16171. 20536 3.60e12 1.76e8 2 2 # ℹ 2 more variables: min_cap_dist , sum_igos ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} trade_exp2012[1,] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 1 × 10 country year imports exports gdp pop land_borders sea_borders 1 China 2012 2331123. 2494240. 1.79e13 1310926531. 14 4 # ℹ 2 more variables: min_cap_dist , sum_igos ``` ::: ::: ## {.center} ## Relationships {.center} . . . `summarize()` lets us calculate statistics for the whole dataset . . . But what if we want to do this for different *groups* within the data? . . . This is at the heart of making **comparisons** ## Variation in trade over time: the brutal way How much do country imports **vary** for each country over time? . . . Tedious: `filter()` to calculate standard deviation of imports for each country . . . Starting with China ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} trade %>% filter(country == "China") %>% summarise(var_trade = sd(imports, na.rm = TRUE)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 1 × 1 var_trade 1 659178. ``` ::: ::: ## Variation in trade over time: the brutal way I could go on to another country: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} trade %>% filter(country == "Algeria") %>% summarise(var_trade = sd(imports, na.rm = TRUE)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 1 × 1 var_trade 1 14819. ``` ::: ::: ## Variation in trade over time: the brutal way And so on for all countries... but this is brutally tedious ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} trade %>% filter(country == "Nepal") %>% summarise(var_trade = sd(imports, na.rm = TRUE)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 1 × 1 var_trade 1 2096. ``` ::: ::: ## Using `group_by()` We can use `group_by()` in combination with `summarize()` to calculate some stat **by** another variable in the data: . . . ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} trade %>% group_by(country) %>% summarise(var_trade = sd(imports, na.rm = TRUE)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 202 × 2 country var_trade 1 Afghanistan 2485. 2 Albania 1754. 3 Algeria 14819. 4 Andorra NA 5 Angola 7584. 6 Antigua & Barbuda NA 7 Argentina 19530. 8 Armenia 1575. 9 Australia 72784. 10 Austria 58474. # ℹ 192 more rows ``` ::: ::: ## Using `group_by()` Like with anything else, we can store our results as a new object: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} var_time = trade %>% group_by(country) %>% summarise(var_trade = sd(imports, na.rm = TRUE)) var_time ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 202 × 2 country var_trade 1 Afghanistan 2485. 2 Albania 1754. 3 Algeria 14819. 4 Andorra NA 5 Angola 7584. 6 Antigua & Barbuda NA 7 Argentina 19530. 8 Armenia 1575. 9 Australia 72784. 10 Austria 58474. # ℹ 192 more rows ``` ::: ::: ## Variation in trade over time And use our object to make plots: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-5_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-37-1.png){fig-align='center' width=100%} ::: ::: ::: notes What does this graph tell us? ::: ## Using `group_by()` We can even `group_by()` multiple variables 🤯 . . . Average life expectancy per **time**: . . . ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} gapminder %>% group_by(year) %>% summarise(avg_life = mean(lifeExp)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 12 × 2 year avg_life 1 1952 49.1 2 1957 51.5 3 1962 53.6 4 1967 55.7 5 1972 57.6 6 1977 59.6 7 1982 61.5 8 1987 63.2 9 1992 64.2 10 1997 65.0 11 2002 65.7 12 2007 67.0 ``` ::: ::: ## Using `group_by()` We can even `group_by()` multiple variables 🤯 . . . Average life expectancy per **year** and **continent**: . . . ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} gapminder %>% group_by(year, continent) %>% summarise(avg_life = mean(lifeExp)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 60 × 3 # Groups: year [12] year continent avg_life 1 1952 Africa 39.1 2 1952 Americas 53.3 3 1952 Asia 46.3 4 1952 Europe 64.4 5 1952 Oceania 69.3 6 1957 Africa 41.3 7 1957 Americas 56.0 8 1957 Asia 49.3 9 1957 Europe 66.7 10 1957 Oceania 70.3 # ℹ 50 more rows ``` ::: ::: ## Using `group_by()` Store as object: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} life_continent_year = gapminder %>% group_by(year, continent) %>% summarise(avg_life = mean(lifeExp)) ``` ::: ## Make the plot ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(life_continent_year, aes(x = year, y = avg_life, color = continent)) + geom_line(size = 2, alpha = .8) + labs(x = "Year", y = "Average life expectancy") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lec-5_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-41-1.png){fig-align='center' width=80%} ::: ::: ## 🚨 Your turn: 🌡️ ugly prejudice 🌡️ 🚨 . . . Researchers use *feeling thermometers* to measure how people feel about different groups . . . 🌡 goes from zero (strong dislike) to 100 (strong like), with 50 as the neutral point . . . ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} | birth_year|sex |race |party_id |educ | ft_black| ft_white| ft_hisp| ft_asian| ft_muslim| ft_jew| ft_christ| ft_fem| ft_immig| ft_gays| ft_unions| ft_police| ft_altright| ft_evang| ft_dem| ft_rep| |----------:|:------|:-----|:-----------|:--------------------|--------:|--------:|-------:|--------:|---------:|------:|---------:|------:|--------:|-------:|---------:|---------:|-----------:|--------:|------:|------:| | 1931|Female |White |Democrat |4-year | 51| 50| 79| 50| 50| 50| 50| 99| 95| 50| 80| 76| 1| 50| 88| 21| | 1952|Female |White |Republican |2-year | 98| 90| 95| 100| 61| 100| 98| 65| 96| 82| 62| 95| 50| 96| 86| 96| | 1931|Male |White |Independent |High school graduate | 87| 90| 91| 88| 49| 25| 50| 74| 77| 77| 100| 78| 0| 2| 91| 20| | 1952|Male |White |Republican |4-year | 90| 85| 90| 96| 80| 91| 94| 25| 91| 71| 20| 94| 50| 70| 22| 83| | 1939|Female |White |Democrat |2-year | 100| 50| 100| 100| 100| 100| 28| 100| 100| 100| 100| 28| NA| NA| 99| NA| | 1959|Female |Black |Democrat |Post-grad | 98| 70| 99| 100| 100| 100| 100| 73| 100| 54| 80| 24| 4| 53| 53| 4| ::: ::: ## 🚨 Your turn: 🌡️ ugly prejudice 🌡️ 🚨 Using the `therm` dataset: 1. Calculate average 🌡 towards **two** groups of your choosing, broken down **by** one respondent characteristic. Make sense of the finding. 2. Calculate average 🌡 towards **two** groups of your choosing, broken down **by** two (🤯🤯🤯) respondent characteristics. Make sense of the finding. 3. Tell the class what you found. ::: callout-tip 1. `?therm` to look at the the variables and `distinct()` to look at a variables values ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html} ``` ::: :::

include-after-body:
<script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
